exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.title: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.passage: What (1) ...... the phrase 'the Scottish Highlands' mean to most people? If you ask someone (2) ...... the top of their head, many will think of (3) ...... on a long trip through misty glens and ancient castles. These days, travellers who want to (4) ...... the real wilderness of Scotland (5) ...... often surprised by just how untouched it still feels. In recent years, local tourism boards (6) ...... worked hard to promote the region, hoping that a (7) ...... of approach will bring visitors back in greater numbers. But those that (8) ...... make the journey will find that highland Scotland differs enormously (9) ...... what many people expect. The area has had to keep (10) ...... with rising visitor expectations, but that's exactly what it (11) ...... doing. It's the first time so much attention (12) ...... been paid to improving access routes. A brand new ferry service (13) ...... that even the most remote islands are now within reach. And it seems (14) ...... be attracting visitors all year round rather than just (15) ...... the summer months.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.title: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

items.sentence: The (PHOTOGRAPH) ...... spent three months travelling across Scotland capturing images of its landscapes and wildlife.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is person who takes photos, so the correct answer is "photographer".

wrongOptions.photograph: The root PHOTOGRAPH cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "photographer".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of PHOTOGRAPH that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: PHOTOGRAPH → photographer: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "photographer" is the word-formation answer from the root PHOTOGRAPH in this context.

items.sentence: Can you tell me which (DIRECT) ...... the lake is in? I think I've got completely turned around.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is which way, so the correct answer is "direction".

wrongOptions.direct: The root DIRECT cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "direction".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of DIRECT that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: DIRECT → direction: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "direction" is the word-formation answer from the root DIRECT in this context.

items.sentence: The town centre has been so extensively renovated that it is now almost (RECOGNISE) ...... compared to ten years ago.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is negative adjective, so the correct answer is "unrecognisable".

wrongOptions.recognise: The root RECOGNISE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "unrecognisable".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of RECOGNISE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: RECOGNISE → unrecognisable: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "unrecognisable" is the word-formation answer from the root RECOGNISE in this context.

items.sentence: Let's check the train (TIME) ...... before we leave the hotel so we don't miss the last service.

wrongOptions.time: The root TIME cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "timetable".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of TIME that fits grammatically, not just the root.

explanationContent.wordFormation: TIME → timetable: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "timetable" is the word-formation answer from the root TIME in this context.

wrongOptions.tour: The root TOUR cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "tourism".

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete each second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete each second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
The pilot asked everyone to disembark immediately after we had boarded. **just**
We ...... the plane when the pilot told us to get off again.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past perfect + just, so the correct answer is "had just got on".

wrongOptions.just got on: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple only shows one past event — use past perfect when one past action happened before another.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect describes an action that happened before another past action.

items.sentence:
Two days have passed since we arrived in Budapest. **for**
We ...... two days.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is duration, so the correct answer is "have been in Budapest for".

wrongOptions.been in budapest for: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
I have only been to Paris on one previous occasion. **second**
This is the ...... Paris.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is ordinal visit, so the correct answer is "second time I've visited".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "second time I've visited", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Matching my husband's walking pace on hiking holidays is something I find very difficult. **up**
I find it hard to ...... my husband on walking holidays.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is match someone's pace, so the correct answer is "keep up with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "keep up with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Travelling has always been Joan's greatest passion, and she has visited countless destinations. **keen**
Joan has always been very ...... and has been everywhere.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is passion for travel, so the correct answer is "keen on travelling".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "keen on travelling", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
We left in the car as fast as we possibly could, ready to start our adventure. **full**
We drove away ...... ready to begin our adventure.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is as fast as possible, so the correct answer is "at full speed".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "at full speed", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
When the storm broke, we made our way towards the old house for shelter. **direction**
As the storm started, we went ...... the old house.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is towards, so the correct answer is "in the direction of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "in the direction of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I wish I had inspected the hotel room before making the reservation. **regret**
I ...... the hotel room before I booked it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past regret, so the correct answer is "regret not looking at".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "regret not looking at", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.A: "ahead" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "advance" fits better.
wrongOptions.ahead: "ahead" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "advance" fits better.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the words before and after the gap together — collocations are heard as a single chunk.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Fixed phrases must be learned as whole chunks — individual words may not translate literally.
